title: Ukraine Burns Moscow's Refineries as Russia Rations Fuel at Home
subhead: Ukraine's drone campaign has knocked out roughly a third of Russian refining capacity, forcing gasoline rationing in Moscow. Meanwhile, Hormuz traffic remains cautious as the US-Iran deal's fine print raises questions about transit tolls and mine clearance timelines.

sections.heading: Ukraine Drones Force Fuel Rationing in Moscow
sections.body:
Ukraine struck the Gazprom Neft Moscow refinery on June 18 in what may have been the largest drone attack on Moscow since the war began, with the city's mayor reporting 180 drones shot down over the capital. The refinery, which normally processes 11 million metric tons of crude per year, was hit for the second time in a week. Video footage showed fireballs and black smoke over the Kapotnya district, roughly 16 km from the Kremlin.

The strike deepened an already acute fuel crisis across Russia. According to RFE/RL, scores of Russian regions have imposed limits on fuel purchases, with motorists restricted to as little as 20-30 liters per vehicle at stations operated by Rosneft, Lukoil, and Tatneft. Tatneft has imposed a 30-liter nationwide limit across its stations. The Taneco refinery in Nizhnekamsk, one of Russia's largest, was also struck on June 12, compounding the damage.

OilPrice reports that the Moscow and Taneco outages have removed roughly 600,000 barrels per day of refining capacity. Energy Intelligence, cited by RFE/RL, estimates that approximately one-third of Russia's total refining capacity has been knocked offline by Ukrainian drone strikes. Russian refining runs are at their lowest level in 21 years, according to industry data. The Kremlin has responded by lowering fuel quality standards, banning gasoline exports through July, and redirecting supplies to priority consumers. Russia, one of the world's largest crude exporters, is now importing gasoline through western ports from Asia while rationing fuel at home.
sections.whyItMatters: The war has come full circle: a petrostate that built its power on energy exports is now rationing gasoline in its own capital and importing fuel from Asia. Ukraine's drone campaign has exposed a structural vulnerability that no amount of crude reserves can fix if you cannot refine it.

sections.heading: Hormuz Traffic Trickles Back, But Questions Mount Over Iran's Terms
sections.body:
Oil shipments through the Strait of Hormuz picked up on June 19 following the US-Iran memorandum of understanding, but the recovery remains cautious and uneven. Reuters reports that while some tankers moved through the strait, shipowners are still erring on the side of safety, with Bloomberg noting that traffic thinned again on Friday as operators assessed the fine print.

The New York Times reports that ships stranded during the closure had hoped to exit quickly after the deal, but faced uncertainty on Friday as the practical details of transit remained unresolved. Mine clearance remains a major bottleneck: Reuters reported that scouring the Strait of Hormuz for mines could take weeks, according to shipping and maritime security sources. Japanese shippers, in particular, have been cautious about resuming voyages without full clearance confirmation.

Under the MOU, Iran pledged to allow free passage for 60 days while negotiations continue. But Tehran has signaled it intends to charge transit tolls in coordination with Oman once that period expires — a move the CFR's Max Boot calls a violation of the freedom of navigation principle the US has upheld since the Barbary Wars. The CFR analysis estimates the total cost of the Iran war to US consumers and taxpayers at roughly $132 billion, with gasoline having peaked at an average of $4.56 per gallon.
sections.whyItMatters: The strait is open in theory, not yet in practice. The 60-day toll-free window is a ceasefire, not a resolution. If Iran follows through on transit fees, every barrel passing through Hormuz would carry a geopolitical surcharge — setting a precedent that other chokepoint operators may copy.

sections.heading: India Lands First Post-Deal LNG Cargo Through Hormuz
sections.body:
India received its first LNG tanker through the Strait of Hormuz since the US-Iran deal on June 18. The LNG carrier Disha docked at Petronet LNG's Dahej terminal in Gujarat carrying 62,370 tonnes of Qatari LNG, according to OilPrice and Indian media. The vessel is the first Indian LNG carrier to transit the strait since the closure began.

India had been particularly exposed to the Hormuz disruption. The New York Times reported that India was eager to restore Iranian oil imports and had been pushing for a diplomatic resolution to the strait closure. The successful transit of the Disha is a tangible sign that commercial flows are resuming, albeit slowly. Iran denied reports this week that it had reimposed a closure, insisting maritime traffic was operating normally — a claim that commercial operators are treating with skepticism given the ongoing mine clearance operations.
sections.whyItMatters: India's first successful post-deal LNG transit is a data point, not a trend. One cargo does not undo months of disrupted supply. But it signals that commercial confidence is returning — and that Qatar, at least, is willing to send ships through before full mine clearance is complete.

sections.heading: OPEC Cuts 2026 Demand Forecast Again as Iran War Drags on Consumption
sections.body:
OPEC lowered its 2026 global oil demand growth forecast for the second consecutive month on June 11, cutting the projection to 970,000 barrels per day — down from 1.17 million bpd in its previous assessment, according to Reuters and OPEC's Monthly Oil Market Report. The group continues to see a smaller demand impact from the Iran war than other forecasters: both the EIA and IEA expect oil demand to decline this year.

For 2027, OPEC raised its demand growth forecast to 1.73 million bpd, up 190,000 bpd from the prior estimate, reflecting an expected rebound once the Hormuz disruption normalizes. OPEC+ crude output averaged 33.13 million bpd in May, down 190,000 bpd from April, with Iran posting the biggest drop due to the US blockade of its exports. The May figure includes the UAE, which left OPEC and OPEC+ on May 1.

The demand downgrade underscores a growing divergence among forecasters. OPEC's 970,000 bpd growth projection assumes the Hormuz disruption eases relatively quickly; the IEA and EIA, which see outright demand contraction this year, are pricing in a more prolonged impact from the war's shock to global trade and consumer spending.
sections.whyItMatters: OPEC's second straight downgrade is a signal: even the cartel's own analysts can no longer pretend the Iran war is a minor bump. The 200,000 bpd revision is significant, but the real story is the gap between OPEC's modest growth forecast and the IEA/EIA expectation of outright demand decline.

sections.heading: Repsol Expands in Venezuela Under New US Sanctions License
sections.body:
Repsol signed a new agreement with Venezuela's Hydrocarbons Ministry and state-owned PdVSA on June 19, expanding its footprint in the country. The deal covers assessment of the Horcon area southeast of Lake Maracaibo — located between existing Repsol-operated fields — and advances offshore gas exploration opportunities. Rigzone reports that the signing was witnessed by Venezuelan Acting President Delcy Rodriguez.

The agreement builds on an April framework under which Repsol committed to growing its Venezuelan gross production by 50 percent within 12 months and tripling it over three years. Repsol currently produces about 45,000 barrels per day in Venezuela, mainly from the Petroquiriquire joint venture. The company will reassume operational control of that field, where it holds a 40% stake alongside PdVSA's 60%.

Critically, the agreement operates under US Treasury General License 50A, issued February 18 after the US captured then-Venezuelan leader Nicolas Maduro. The license requires that any oil or gas royalties owed to the Venezuelan government or PdVSA be paid into accounts designated by the US government — not to Caracas directly. The license also covers BP, Shell, Chevron, Eni, and Maurel & Prom.
sections.whyItMatters: GL50A is quietly reshaping Venezuela's oil sector. Western companies are returning under a framework that funnels government revenue through US-controlled accounts — a form of economic guardianship that gives Washington leverage over Caracas while letting production grow.

sections.heading: Ukraine's Drone Strategy Shifts From Refineries to Supply Lines
sections.body:
CNN reports that a new generation of Ukrainian mid-range drones is severely disrupting Russian military supply lines to the southern front, going beyond the refinery strikes that have dominated headlines. While refinery attacks have knocked out roughly one-third of Russia's domestic refining capacity, the new drone campaign targets logistics nodes, fuel depots, and supply convoys — hitting the Russian military's ability to sustain operations rather than just its fuel supply at home.

The dual-track approach — strategic strikes on refineries that ration Moscow's gasoline, plus tactical interdiction of military logistics — marks a significant evolution in Ukraine's drone warfare. Russian officials have warned of 'massive retaliation' for the refinery attacks, with Putin threatening escalation, but the tactical effect is undeniable: RFE/RL reports fuel shortages spreading from occupied Crimea and Donetsk into Russia proper, with regions across the country imposing purchase limits.

The strikes also came at an awkward moment for the Kremlin: the Tatarstan strikes hit just before Putin hosted Southeast Asian leaders at a Russia-ASEAN summit in Kazan on June 18, and earlier attacks disrupted his annual investment forum in St. Petersburg.
sections.whyItMatters: Ukraine is no longer just hitting symbols — it is degrading Russia's ability to fight a war and run a country simultaneously. The fuel rationing in Moscow is a strategic dividend of the refinery campaign, but the shift to military logistics interdiction targets the operational core of Russia's war effort.

sections.heading: Central Banks Press Ahead With Rate Hikes Despite Iran Deal
sections.body:
The Iran war's inflationary shock is proving stickier than the peace deal can fix. Reuters reports that the Iran deal's oil price relief is not stopping central banks from raising borrowing costs, with the inflation surge from the Hormuz closure already embedded in price data. The Bank of Japan hiked rates this week, with core CPI holding steady at 1.4% year-on-year in May — held down by government energy subsidies, which mask the underlying pressure.

Japan Times reports that core consumer prices matched expectations, with subsidies restraining the energy component. But former BOJ policymakers told Reuters the bank may raise rates twice more by March 2027, reflecting confidence that inflationary pressure will persist even as oil prices ease. In the US, the Federal Reserve held rates steady this week under new chair Kevin Warsh, but signaled rising borrowing costs later this year, with the 2-year Treasury note hitting its highest level in over a year.

G7 leaders in Evian-les-Bains welcomed the Iran framework deal, but the summit was dominated by the reality that the war's economic damage — fertilizer prices up 47%, global growth downgraded to 2.5% by the World Bank, depleted munitions stockpiles — will take months or years to unwind, regardless of what happens next in the Strait.
sections.whyItMatters: The Iran deal calms the geopolitical temperature, but the economic fever has already set in. Central banks are hiking into a recovery because the war's inflationary damage has already been absorbed into wages, prices, and expectations. The deal is a necessary condition for stabilization, not a sufficient one.

bottomLine: Ukraine's drone campaign has achieved something few thought possible: forcing gasoline rationing in Moscow and forcing Russia to import fuel from Asia. One-third of Russian refining capacity is offline, and the strikes are now extending from economic targets to military supply lines. Meanwhile, the Hormuz reopening is proceeding in fits and starts — India's first post-deal LNG cargo is a positive signal, but mine clearance will take weeks, and Iran's plans for transit tolls after the 60-day window mean the strait's 'reopening' comes with an asterisk. OPEC's second straight demand downgrade and central banks' determination to keep hiking despite the deal confirm that the war's economic damage is baked in, not transitory.
sources: Reuters, RFE/RL, OilPrice, Bloomberg, CNN, New York Times, CFR (Max Boot), Japan Times, Rigzone, Energy Intelligence, OPEC Monthly Oil Market Report, CNBC, MSN, NHK World, Japan Times
